title: Emissions of air pollutants
subtitle: Air pollutants are gases that can lead to negative impacts on human health and ecosystems. Most are produced from energy, industry, and agriculture.
caption:
Emissions of air pollutants

Air pollutants are gases that can lead to negative impacts on human health and ecosystems. Most are produced from energy, industry, and agriculture.

This composite chart displays six line graphs tracking global emissions of specific air pollutants from 1750 to 2022. The vertical axes measure emissions in tonnes, with scales varying by pollutant. Nitrogen oxides, sulfur dioxide, and non-methane volatile organic compounds share a scale reaching 100 million tonnes, while carbon monoxide reaches 600 million tonnes, ammonia reaches 60 million tonnes, and black carbon reaches 6 million tonnes. Across all six categories, emissions remain near the baseline from 1750 until the mid-19th century, after which they begin a gradual ascent. A sharp acceleration occurs in all graphs starting around 1950. Nitrogen oxides rise steeply to a peak exceeding 100 million tonnes around 2010 before declining slightly. Sulfur dioxide follows a similar trajectory, peaking above 100 million tonnes in the late 20th century and showing a more pronounced drop toward 2022. Carbon monoxide emissions climb steadily to a peak near 600 million tonnes around 2000, followed by a decrease. Black carbon and ammonia show continuous upward trends throughout the timeline, with black carbon reaching approximately 6 million tonnes and ammonia exceeding 60 million tonnes by 2022. Non-methane volatile organic compounds rise to over 100 million tonnes by the early 21st century and plateau near that level through 2022. The visual data indicates that while most pollutants peaked in the late 20th or early 21st century, ammonia and black carbon continue to rise or remain at their highest recorded levels at the end of the period.
